title: Detecting nonclassicality in randomly-displaced copies of a squeezed state
abstract: We address a fundamental question: Can one determine whether a received signal is squeezed when each copy arrives with a different displacement/amplitude? We introduce an interaction Hamiltonian that converts quadrature squeezing into number squeezing. Using this conversion, we test whether the copies satisfy $g^{(2)}(0)<1$. The Hamiltonian itself does not create nonclassicality; it only transfers it from quadrature squeezing to number squeezing. This allows us to identify squeezing even when individual copies have random displacements.
summary: The paper presents a method to identify quadrature squeezing even when the signal's amplitude fluctuates randomly between pulses. By using a specific interaction to convert quadrature squeezing into number squeezing, the authors show that the nonclassical signature becomes detectable and remains invariant under signal attenuation.
main_problem: How to detect quadrature squeezing in a signal when each received copy has a different, unknown random displacement due to environmental noise or channel fluctuations.
main_result: The authors propose an interaction Hamiltonian that converts quadrature squeezing into number squeezing, allowing for detection via the second-order correlation function g(2)(0) which remains robust against random displacements and attenuation.
method: The paper uses an operator formalism (Pegg-Barnett) and the Baker-Campbell-Hausdorff lemma to derive a beam-splitter-like transformation that maps quadrature variables to number-phase variables, supplemented by numerical simulations in a finite Fock space.
model_or_system: A two-mode system consisting of an incident squeezed mode and a number-phase mode, with potential implementations in circuit QED (Josephson junctions), atomic ensembles (Raman transitions), and nonlinear optics (Kerr nonlinearities).
key_observables: Second-order correlation function g(2)(0), normalized number variance, and the g(2)(tau) histogram.
important_parameters: Large photon number regime (n >> 1), squeezing rates, displacement amplitudes, and transmission coefficient (eta).
main_assumptions: The large-occupation approximation is used for the validity of the number-phase commutation relation, and the environment/loss mode is assumed to be in the vacuum state.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the detection protocol schematic (displacement, conversion, and detection); Figure 2 illustrates the conversion of quadrature squeezing to number squeezing; Figure 3 shows the enhancement of signal visibility via counter-displacement.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of random displacements, defines the conversion Hamiltonian and its operator properties, proves the non-generation of nonclassicality, demonstrates the conversion mechanism and entanglement generation, provides an analytical proof of g(2)(0) invariance under loss, and discusses physical implementations.
why_interesting: It provides a robust protocol for quantum state verification in noisy, fluctuating channels, which is critical for quantum communication and sensing in real-world environments like atmospheric links.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Protocol for detecting nonclassicality in randomly displaced copies of a squeezed state. (i) Alice sends identical copies of a squeezed state ˆρ0, but each copy reaches the receiver as ˆρt with an unknown displacement due to attenuation in the dynamical channel. (ii) The received copy is displaced by ˆD(α) with a large α, i.e., |α|2 ≫1, to obtain ˆρa. The operation ˆD(α) does not change the nonclassicality of ˆρt [6, 7, 23, 24], but the resulting states ˆρa have much narrower pulse-to-pulse variations in their mean photon number. (iii) ˆρa enters the ˆBn(θ) operation, which converts the quadrature squeezing in ˆρa into number squeezing in the output state ˆρb (see Fig. 2a). As a...

FIG. 2. Transformation of initial quadrature squeezing into number-squeezing (g(2)(0) < 1) under the action of the ˆBn(θ) operator. (a) Nonclassicality of the second (ˆbn) mode in- creases as g(2)(0) decreases. (b) Quadrature squeezing of the first (ˆa) mode decreases, with the uncertainty (∆ˆxa) increasing. One type of nonclassicality (quadrature squeez- ing) present in the first mode is converted into another type (number-squeezing) in the second mode.

FIG. 3. While the number-squeezed ˆbn output mode possesses a ⟨(∆ˆn)2⟩/⟨ˆn⟩ratio much below the unity, the g(2)(0) stays close to unity because of the large occupation. We perform a counter-displacement in the ˆbn output mode before we send them to the g(2)(τ) histogram. The ˆD(α′) displacement does not generate nonclassicality from coherent state ˆbn outputs, however it makes g(2)(0) < 1 states more visible.
